title: CSR & Stakeholder Theory
simple_html:
Who does a company owe duties to? The old answer was simple: its shareholders, the people who own it. Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) and stakeholder theory argue the answer is broader — a firm also has responsibilities to employees, customers, suppliers, communities, and the environment.

Corporate Social Responsibility means a business voluntarily taking responsibility for its impact on society, beyond just obeying the law and making profit. The classic map is Carroll's CSR pyramid, which stacks four layers of responsibility: economic (be profitable — the foundation), legal (obey the law), ethical (do what is right, fair, and just even when not required), and philanthropic (be a good corporate citizen, give back).

The big debate is between two views. Milton Friedman argued the only social responsibility of business is to increase profits within the rules — anything else is spending other people's money. Stakeholder theory (Edward Freeman) replies that a firm is a web of relationships, and creating value for all stakeholders is both more ethical and better for long-term success.

Key takeaways

  • CSR = responsibility to society beyond law and profit.
  • Carroll's pyramid: economic, legal, ethical, philanthropic.
  • Friedman: maximize shareholder value within the law.
  • Stakeholder theory: serve all who affect or are affected by the firm.
  • Modern ideas (Triple Bottom Line, ESG, Shared Value) try to measure and combine profit with social and environmental performance.
intermediate_html:
This lesson tackles the central question of corporate purpose: for whom does the corporation exist? It frames the long-running shareholder-versus-stakeholder debate and surveys the practical models managers use to operationalize social responsibility.

Carroll's pyramid of CSR

Archie Carroll (1991) organized corporate responsibilities into four hierarchical layers: economic (be profitable — the bedrock that makes the rest possible), legal (comply with laws and regulations), ethical (act fairly and avoid harm beyond legal minimums), and philanthropic (contribute resources to community welfare; "desired" rather than "required"). The pyramid is intuitive and exam-friendly, but note its nuance: the layers are meant to be fulfilled simultaneously, not sequentially, and critics argue the hierarchy implies ethics is subordinate to profit. In collectivist cultures the ordering may differ.

The Friedman doctrine vs. stakeholder theory

Milton Friedman (1970), in his famous New York Times essay, argued that "the social responsibility of business is to increase its profits" — managers are agents of shareholders and have no mandate to spend corporate funds on social causes (that is a hidden tax). Crucially, Friedman did include the constraint "while conforming to the basic rules of the society, both those embodied in law and those embodied in ethical custom."

R. Edward Freeman (1984) countered with stakeholder theory: a stakeholder is any group that can affect or is affected by the firm's objectives. The firm should be managed for the benefit of all primary stakeholders (employees, customers, suppliers, communities, financiers), not shareholders alone. Freeman offers three justifications: descriptive (this is how firms actually work), instrumental (managing stakeholders well drives performance), and normative (stakeholders have intrinsic legitimate interests).

Stakeholder salience

Because not all stakeholders can be served equally, Mitchell, Agle & Wood (1997) proposed stakeholder salience: managers prioritize stakeholders by three attributes — power (ability to impose will), legitimacy (a valid claim), and urgency (time-sensitivity). A stakeholder holding all three is "definitive" and demands immediate attention; holding one is merely "latent." Salience is dynamic — a community group can gain urgency and power overnight through media or activism.

Contemporary models

ModelCore idea
Triple Bottom Line (Elkington, 1994)Measure People, Planet, Profit — three bottom lines, not one.
Creating Shared Value (Porter & Kramer, 2011)Solve social problems in ways that also generate competitive advantage — CSR as strategy, not charity.
ESGEnvironmental, Social, Governance metrics used by investors to assess non-financial risk and performance.

Worked mini-case

A clothing retailer discovers a supplier uses unsafe factories. Friedman lens: act only if legally required or if reputational damage threatens profit. Stakeholder lens: workers and communities are legitimate stakeholders; the firm should remediate conditions even at cost. Salience analysis: workers have legitimacy and (post-disaster) urgency but little power; NGOs and the media supply the power, raising salience and forcing action. Shared Value lens: investing in supplier safety and training reduces disruption risk and strengthens the brand — doing good and doing well converge.

How to apply it

Build a stakeholder map (identify, then rate power/legitimacy/urgency), align CSR initiatives with the firm's strategy and core competencies (avoid disconnected philanthropy), and report transparently against TBL/ESG metrics — while staying alert to greenwashing, where claims outrun substance.
full_html:
This lesson confronts the foundational question of corporate purpose and the obligations that flow from it. It is the bridge between individual decision-making (Lesson 6) and the institutional architecture of Lesson 8, and it crystallizes a debate that has shaped corporate law, investment, and strategy for over half a century: should the corporation be run for its shareholders or for a broader set of stakeholders?

Defining CSR and Carroll's pyramid

Corporate Social Responsibility refers to a firm's obligations to society beyond profit-making and legal compliance. The canonical structuring is Archie Carroll's (1979; refined 1991) four-part model: economic responsibilities (be profitable — the foundation), legal (obey the law), ethical (do what is right and fair even absent legal compulsion), and philanthropic (be a good corporate citizen). Carroll later integrated this with stakeholder theory to specify to whom each responsibility is owed. Important nuances: the layers are to be met concurrently; the pyramid's profit-first base has been criticized for implying ethics is negotiable; and the philanthropic tier's status as merely "desired" weakens it. Visser (2006) argues that for developing economies the ordering should be reweighted, with philanthropy and economic development more prominent.

The shareholder doctrine: Friedman and agency theory

Milton Friedman (1970) gave the shareholder view its sharpest statement: in a free society, "there is one and only one social responsibility of business — to use its resources and engage in activities designed to increase its profits so long as it stays within the rules of the game." His argument rests on agency theory (later formalized by Jensen & Meckling, 1976): executives are agents of the shareholder-principals; deploying corporate funds for social ends the shareholders did not authorize is spending other people's money — effectively taxation without representation. The view aligns with the legal doctrine of shareholder primacy (e.g., Dodge v. Ford, 1919). Note the frequently overlooked ethical constraint in Friedman's own formulation: profit-seeking must conform to "law" and "ethical custom" — Friedman was not endorsing fraud or deception.

Stakeholder theory

R. Edward Freeman's Strategic Management: A Stakeholder Approach (1984) reframed the firm as a nexus of relationships among groups that "can affect or are affected by" its objectives. Donaldson & Preston (1995) clarified that stakeholder theory operates on three distinct levels — descriptive (it models how corporations actually behave), instrumental (good stakeholder management correlates with performance), and normative (stakeholders possess intrinsic worth and legitimate interests, grounded ultimately in Kantian respect for persons; see Lesson 3). The normative core is the theory's ethical heart and its strongest claim. Critics (e.g., Jensen, 2002) counter that serving multiple masters with no single objective function leaves managers unaccountable; Jensen's enlightened value maximization tries to reconcile the camps by treating long-run firm value as the scorecard while taking stakeholder interests seriously as inputs.

Stakeholder identification and salience

Mitchell, Agle & Wood (1997) addressed the practical problem of prioritization with their stakeholder salience model: stakeholders are classified by power, legitimacy, and urgency. Combinations yield latent (one attribute), expectant (two), and definitive (all three) stakeholders. The model's dynamism is essential — attributes are socially constructed and shift rapidly, as when a previously ignored community group acquires power and urgency through coalition with media and NGOs (the Rana Plaza aftermath being a vivid case).

Beyond the binary: integrative models

Several frameworks attempt to transcend the shareholder/stakeholder dichotomy. John Elkington's (1994) Triple Bottom Line proposes accounting for People, Planet, and Profit; Elkington himself later "recalled" the concept (2018), warning it had been reduced to a watered-down reporting exercise rather than driving system change. Michael Porter & Mark Kramer's (2011) Creating Shared Value (CSV) argues firms should generate economic value by addressing societal needs, locating CSR inside competitive strategy; critics (Crane et al., 2014) charge that CSV is naive about genuine trade-offs and merely repackages existing ideas. ESG investing operationalizes these concerns into Environmental, Social, and Governance metrics, though it faces measurement inconsistency and greenwashing risks.

Worked example

A pharmaceutical firm holds a patent on a drug critical in low-income countries. Friedman/agency: maximize licensed revenue within the law; donations require shareholder sanction. Stakeholder/normative: patients are legitimate stakeholders with a compelling (life-or-death) urgency; the firm has duties beyond contract. Salience: patients have legitimacy and urgency but limited power until NGOs and governments amplify them. CSV: tiered pricing plus local manufacturing can expand the market, build reputation, and pre-empt compulsory licensing — converting an apparent profit/ethics trade-off into shared value. Carroll: the economic tier is satisfied by tiered pricing; the ethical tier requires not exploiting desperate need.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Caricaturing Friedman as endorsing amorality — he explicitly bounded profit-seeking with law and ethical custom.
  • Treating CSR as philanthropy/PR bolted on after the fact, disconnected from strategy and easily cut in downturns.
  • Assuming a guaranteed business case — the CSR-financial-performance link is positive but modest and contingent (Margolis & Walsh, 2003); overselling it invites cynicism.
  • Ignoring trade-offs — shared-value optimism can mask real conflicts between stakeholder interests.
  • Greenwashing — claims exceeding substance, eroding trust.

Links to the syllabus

The normative justification for stakeholder duties draws on Kantian rights (Lesson 3) and Rawlsian justice (Lesson 5); the failure modes of CSR programs connect to moral disengagement (Lesson 6); and the accountability problem raised by stakeholder theory leads directly into corporate governance and agency mechanisms (Lesson 8).
